name: saintool

metadata.name: SAInTool
metadata.summary: SAInT: An Interactive Tool for Sensitivity Analysis In The Loop

documentation:
# SAInT: An Interactive Tool for Sensitivity Analysis In The Loop.

We introduce SAInT, an Interactive Tool for Sensitivity Analysis in The Loop, which enables users to train, evaluate, visualize, and explain Machine Learning (ML) models using a graphical interface. Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) tools support informed decision-making through successive iterations with human knowledge. Sensitivity Analysis (SA) is an Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) technique that provides additional insights into model behavior. A key challenge is that using ML typically requires programming skills, especially for integrating XAI methods. This creates barriers to efficient model development and hinders interdisciplinary collaboration. To address this challenge, our tool integrates Interactive ML (IML) with Local SA (LSA) and Global SA (GSA), enabling users to gain insights into model behavior without requiring programming skills. SAInT can be used by Artificial Intelligence (AI) researchers and domain experts. Users can tune hyperparameters, detect data biases, gain insights in model decision-making, select the best features, visualize outliers, apply models on other datasets, explore model reliability, and improve data generation for model refinement.

## Overview

SAInT integrates Interactive Machine Learning (a-e) and Sensitivity Analysis (f-h) in a loop with user interactions. a) Feature Selection: Define in- and output features. b) Model Configuration: Configure model parameters. c) Model Training: Train or load models. d) Criteria Selection: Select dataset and loss. e) Evaluation: Error plot of all models. The best model is selected. f) Interactive Plot of the best model: Click onto a sample. g) Local Sensitivity Analysis: Local explanations for the selected sample. h) Global Sensitivity Analysis: Identify the best features, which can be used in a) as refinement.

## Video [![Watch the video](https://img.youtube.com/vi/m269sWdYUUI/maxresdefault.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m269sWdYUUI) If you use our tool, please cite us. ## Installation We offer two options of installing our package. Note: It is recommended to install the package within a virtual environment, for example with: ``` python -m venv myEnv ``` Then activate the virtual environment on Windows with: ``` .\myEnv\Scripts\activate ``` or on Linux with: ``` ./myEnv/bin/activate ``` ### Option 1: Fast and easy Installation using pip (recommended) ``` pip install saintool ``` or ### Option 2: Installation of SAInT using the code of the GitHub Repository Clone the repository using SSH or HTTP or download and unzip the zip package. Clone using SSH: ``` git clone git@github.com:dfki-asr/SAInT.git ``` Clone using HTTP: ``` git clone https://github.com/dfki-asr/SAInT.git ``` Go to the main directory. ``` cd SAInT ``` Install the package by executing the script: ``` bash ./install.sh ``` ## Setup working_directory and folder structure If you installed the tool with **Option 1**, there is no default SAInT main directory. We recommend to create a working directory that will contain two folders ```data``` and ```outputs```: ``` SAInT_working_directory/ ├── data └── outputs ``` If you installed the tool using **Option 2**, the SAInT directory already contains ```data``` and ```outputs``` folders. Create a subfolder for each dataset in the data folder and copy your .csv-data into it. **Single dataset, random splitting**: Placing only a single file will randomly split it into ```train```, ```valid```, and ```test```. Adjust the split fractions in the ```app_settings.json```: ```valid_frac``` and ```test_frac```. **Multiple pre-splitted datasets** Place your data as ```train_data.csv```, ```valid_data.csv``` and ```test_data.csv``` into the subfolder. ``` data/ ├── titanic | └── total_data.csv ├── your_own_dataset │ ├── train_data.csv │ ├── valid_data.csv │ └── test_data.csv ├── ishigami_sobol_g | └── total_data.csv └── ... ``` SAInT will automatically create the output directory for each dataset with ```models``` and ```figures``` subfolders and place the results there: ``` outputs/ ├── titanic │ ├── figures │ └── models ├── your_own_dataset │ ├── figures │ └── models ├── ishigami_sobol_g │ ├── figures │ └── models └── ... ``` ## Start the application Start the SAInT Dash application in the browser: ``` bash ./run.sh ``` or by typing: ``` python3 -m SAInT ``` The default browser should open automatically. Alternatively, you can use a browser of your choice by opening a new browser window and type in the address: http://127.0.0.1:8050/

functions.name: SAInT.metrics.regression.logcosh.logcosh
documentation:
Log cosh error
the original log cosh error is defined as:
    mean(log(cosh(inp-targ)))
it can be infinity with high tensor differences.

The stable log cosh error version:
    mean( (inp-targ) + log(cosh(inp-targ)) - log(2.0))

functions.name: SAInT.metrics.classification.smoothed_hinge.smoothed_hinge_np
documentation: Smoothed Hinge Loss: NumPy implementation
Sources:
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2301.05579,
http://qwone.com/~jason/writing/smoothHinge.pdf

functions.name: SAInT.metrics.classification.smoothed_hinge.smoothed_hinge_torch
documentation: Smoothed Hinge Loss: PyTorch implementation

functions.name: SAInT.metrics.classification.smoothed_hinge.smoothed_hinge
documentation:
Smoothed Hinge Loss
For z ≥ 1       then 0             correct classification with a margin z of at least 1
For 0 < z < 1   then 0.5 (1-z)**2  loss is a quadratic term for smooth transition
For z ≤ 0:      then 0.5 - z       similar to the traditional hinge loss, but it is linear

functions.name: SAInT.metrics.classification.squared_hinge.squared_hinge
documentation:
Quadratically Smoothed (Squared) Hinge Loss
# if z >= 1 - gamma   then   1/(2*gamma) * max(0, -z)
#                     else   1 -  0.5 * gamma - z

methods.name: SAInT.sa.lsa_shap.LocalShapExplainer.get_top_n_features
documentation:
This function returns a list of the top_n most important features, either positively or negatively.
Parameters:
- explanation: the SHAP explanation object
- top_n: the number of top features to consider (default is 5)
Returns:
- List of the top n most important features.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the GlobalExplainer with the provided model and data.

Parameters:
- model: The model to be explained.
- data: The data used for explanation.
- num_samples: Number of samples for analysis.
- figure_folder: Directory to save figures.
- do_show: Boolean to control if figures should be shown.
- do_save: Boolean to control if figures should be saved.
- random_seed: Seed for random number generation.
- epsilon: Small value to adjust bounds when lower and upper bounds are the same.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._adjust_bounds
documentation:
Adjust bounds to ensure lower and upper bounds are not the same.

Parameters:
- lower_bounds: The minimum values for each feature.
- upper_bounds: The maximum values for each feature.
- epsilon: Small value to adjust bounds.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer.analyze
documentation:
Perform sensitivity analysis using the specified method.

Parameters:
- method: The method for analysis ('sobol', 'morris', 'fast', 'rbd-fast').

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._analyze_sobol
documentation: Perform Sobol sensitivity analysis.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._analyze_morris
documentation: Perform Morris sensitivity analysis.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._analyze_fast
documentation: Perform FAST sensitivity analysis.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._analyze_rbd_fast
documentation: Perform RBD-FAST sensitivity analysis.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer.plot
documentation:
Plot the results of the sensitivity analysis.

Parameters:
- title: Title of the plot.
- first_order_color: Color for first order bars.
- total_order_color: Color for total order bars.

Returns:
- ax: The axis object of the plot.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._replace_plot_colors_and_update_legend_labels
documentation:
Replace plot colors and update legend labels.

Parameters:
- ax: The axis object to modify.
- first_order_color: Color for first order bars.
- total_order_color: Color for total order bars.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._save_and_show_plot
documentation:
Save and show the plot based on configuration.

Parameters:
- title: Title of the plot for saving.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer.get_n_most_important_features
documentation:
Get the n most important features from the analysis.

Parameters:
- factor: Factor to scale the median value for threshold.
- num_top_features: Number of top features to return (-1 for all above threshold).

Returns:
- dict: Dictionary of most important features per measure.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._get_sorted_features_for_all_targets
documentation:
Get sorted features for all targets based on a specific measure.

Parameters:
- measure: The measure to sort features by.
- factor: Factor to scale the median value for threshold.
- num_top_features: Number of top features to return (-1 for all above threshold).

Returns:
- list: List of sorted feature names.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._get_feature_names
documentation:
Get feature names from the problem specification.

Parameters:
- target_name: The target name to get feature names for.

Returns:
- list: List of feature names.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._get_sorted_features_per_measure
documentation:
Get sorted features for a specific measure and target.

Parameters:
- measure: The measure to sort features by.
- target_name: The target name to get features for.
- total_feature_names: List of all feature names.
- factor: Factor to scale the median value for threshold.
- num_top_features: Number of top features to return (-1 for all above threshold).

Returns:
- OrderedDict: Sorted features and their values.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._get_measure_values
documentation:
Get values for a specific measure and target.

Parameters:
- analysis: The analysis result dictionary.
- measure: The measure to get values for.
- target_name: The target name to get values for.

Returns:
- np.array: Array of measure values.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer._sort_features_by_importance
documentation:
Sort features by their importance based on measure values.

Parameters:
- values: Array of measure values.
- total_feature_names: List of all feature names.
- factor: Factor to scale the median value for threshold.
- num_top_features: Number of top features to return (-1 for all above threshold).

Returns:
- OrderedDict: Sorted feature names and their importance values.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.gsa.GlobalExplainer.apply_gsa
documentation:
Apply global sensitivity analysis (GSA) to the model.

Parameters:
- method: Method for analysis ('sobol', 'morris', 'fast', 'rbd-fast').
- factor: Factor to scale the median value for threshold.
- num_top_features: Number of top features to return (-1 for all above threshold).
- colors: Colors for first and total order bars.

Returns:
- tuple: dictionary of most important features, and axis object of the plot.

methods.name: SAInT.sa.lsa_lime.LocalLimeExplainer.get_top_n_features
documentation:
This function returns a list of the top_n most important features, either positively or negatively.
Parameters:
- explanation: the LIME explanation object
- top_n: the number of top features to consider (default is 5)
Returns:
- List of the top n most important features.

methods.name: SAInT.data_balancer.DataBalancer.balance_classes
documentation:
Balance classes in the DataFrame based on a specific feature.

```
param dataframe (pd.DataFrame)

Input DataFrame.

param feature (str)

The feature to balance classes on.

param feature_vals (list)

List of feature values to balance.

param balance_factor (float)

The balance factor (default is 0.5).

param random_seed (int)

Random seed for reproducibility (default is 123456).

return

List of selected indices.
```

methods.name: SAInT.data_balancer.DataBalancer.get_balanced_dataset
documentation: Return a balanced dataset based on the specified feature and balance factor.

functions.name: SAInT.common.check_high_correlation
documentation:
Check if any features in the DataFrame are strongly correlated.

Parameters:
df (pd.DataFrame): Input DataFrame
threshold (float): Correlation threshold to consider as strong (default=0.9)

Returns:
None: Prints a warning with strongly correlated features if any are found.

The application instance.

methods.name: SAInT.dash_application.feature_importance.dash_global_explainer.DashGlobalExplainer._perform_gsa_analysis
documentation:
Perform GSA analysis and update the application with the results.

```
param method

The method used for GSA ('fast' by default).

param colors

List of colors used for the GSA plot.

param do_save

Flag to save the GSA figure.

return

The GSA figure.
```

methods.name: SAInT.dash_application.feature_importance.dash_global_explainer.DashGlobalExplainer.explain
documentation:
Explain GSA by performing the analysis and loading the figure.

```
param method

The method used for GSA ('fast' by default).

param colors

List of colors used for the GSA plot.

param do_save

Flag to save the GSA figure.

param width

The width of the figure.

param height

The height of the figure.

return

The source of the loaded SVG.
```

modules.name: SAInT.dash_application.optimizer.examples.kutec_example copy
documentation: import os
import json

functions.name: SAInT.dash_application.optimizer.examples.kutec_example copy.get_quality
documentation: def load_from_json(path: str):
print(f"load_from_json: {path}")
if not os.path.exists(path):
raise RuntimeError(f"{path} does not exist!")
with open(path, 'r') as file:
return json.load(file)
def get_definition(name: str) -> OrderedDict:
current_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
return load_from_json(f"{current_directory}/{name}.json")

functions.name: SAInT.dash_application.optimizer.examples_old.old.kutec_example copy.get_quality
documentation: def load_from_json(path: str):
print(f"load_from_json: {path}")
if not os.path.exists(path):
raise RuntimeError(f"{path} does not exist!")
with open(path, 'r') as file:
return json.load(file)
def get_definition(name: str) -> OrderedDict:
current_directory = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
return load_from_json(f"{current_directory}/{name}.json")

methods.name: SAInT.dataset copy.Dataset.__init__
documentation:

```
param dataframe (pd.DataFrame)

Input DataFrame.

param mode (str)

Mode of the dataset (e.g., 'train', 'valid').

param output_names (list)

List of output feature names.

param normalization_values

Normalization values object.

param features_to_normalize (list)

List of features to normalize.

param normalization (str)

Method: none, mean_std or min_max

param verbose (bool)

Verbosity flag.

param random_seed (int)

Random seed for reproducibility.

param is_normalized (bool)

Flag indicating if the data is normalized.

param categorical_names (list)

List of categorical feature names.

param continuous_names (list)

List of continuous feature names.

param procs (list)

List of preprocessing functions.
```

methods.name: SAInT.dataset copy.Dataset.to_fastai
documentation: Convert Dataset to FastAI's TabularDataLoaders.

functions.name: SAInT.dataset.custom_cont_cat_split
documentation:
Split DataFrame columns into continuous and categorical based on data type and cardinality.

```
param df (pd.DataFrame)

Input DataFrame.

param max_card (int)

Maximum cardinality for categorical features (default is 10).

return

tuple: List of continuous and categorical column names.
```

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the Dataset object.

```
param dataframe (pd.DataFrame)

Input DataFrame.

param mode (str)

Mode of the dataset (e.g., 'train', 'valid').

param output_names (list)

List of output feature names.

param normalization_values

Normalization values object.

param features_to_normalize (list)

List of features to normalize.

param normalization (str)

Method: none, mean_std or min_max

param verbose (bool)

Verbosity flag.

param random_seed (int)

Random seed for reproducibility.

param is_normalized (bool)

Flag indicating if the data is normalized.

param categorical_names (list)

List of categorical feature names.

param continuous_names (list)

List of continuous feature names.

param procs (list)

List of preprocessing functions.
```

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.convert_to_float
documentation: Convert all columns in the DataFrame to a specified float type.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.convert_object_or_bool_dtype
documentation: Convert object and bool columns to appropriate numeric types.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.tabular_pd
documentation: Get a TabularPandas object from the DataFrame.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.is_classification
documentation:
Check if the dataset is for classification.

:param name (str): Name of the target column to check (default is 'Class').
:returns: bool: True if the target column is for classification, False otherwise.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.normalize
documentation: Normalize the DataFrame using the DataNormalizer.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.denormalize
documentation: Denormalize the DataFrame using the DataNormalizer.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.get_balanced_dataset
documentation: Get a balanced dataset based on the specified feature and balance factor.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.replace_inf_by_nan
documentation: Replace infinite values with NaN in the DataFrame.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.drop_nan_entries_and_get_nan_columns
documentation: Drop rows with NaN values and return a list of columns with high NaN percentage.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.drop_features
documentation: Drop specified features from the DataFrame.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.remove_samples
documentation: Remove samples from the DataFrame based on the provided indices.

methods.name: SAInT.dataset.Dataset.onehot_encode
documentation: One-hot encode all categorical features.

documentation: Get FastAI's TabularDataLoaders or TabularPandas object for the dataset.

documentation: Compute the correlation matrix for the input DataFrame.

documentation: Analyze and print statistics (mean, std, bounds) of continuous inputs and outputs.

documentation: Reduce features based on correlation with target variables.

documentation: Reduce the input features to the specified list of features.
